teamObjective.explanation: My hand is all low non-trump spades (3-6). I cannot realistically keep the lead with these cards, so I aim to play the cheapest possible card and let my partner win the trick if they can, transferring control cheaply.

partnerBelief.summary: My partner (player 2) holds 3 unknown cards. With no public play yet, I believe they could have slightly higher non-trump cards or a trump that can win a low lead, but I cannot treat any specific card as a fact.

opponentBelief.summary: Each opponent has 3 unknown cards. They likely hold cards higher than 3, including trumps, so they can probably beat any single I lead. I should minimize the value of the card I sacrifice.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 is the lowest-cost probe. It gives my partner the best chance to win with a modest card and preserves the 4, 5, and 6 for later coverage or as slightly stronger exit cards.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 4 is still low but commits a slightly more useful card; if an opponent beats it, I have lost a card that could have covered a future 3 lead.
The 5 is higher than necessary for a probe and is more likely to be wasted when an opponent plays a trump or higher non-trump.
The 6 is my strongest card and is very unlikely to win the trick outright; playing it now sacrifices my best exit card and leaves me with only weaker follow-ups.

risks: An opponent may beat the 3 and take control, then pressure my team.
My partner may be unable to win the trick, so control may still pass to opponents.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By sacrificing the lowest card, I limit information and card-value leakage. If partner wins, we gain tempo at low cost; if not, I retain marginally better cards for later tricks.

notes: No cards have been played, so all inferences about partner/opponent holdings remain probabilistic.
